name: Prince William County Public Schools
districtFact: Second largest school district in Virginia, serving one of the most rapidly growing and culturally diverse counties in the Washington D.C. suburbs.

about.title: About the District
about.content: Prince William County Public Schools is the second-largest school district in Virginia, serving approximately 90,000 students in one of the fastest-growing counties in the Washington D.C. metropolitan area.

planningTips: PWCS observes Eid al-Fitr (Mar 10), Eid al-Adha (May 17), Yom Kippur (Sep 21), and Juneteenth (Jun 18) as school holidays. Families from other backgrounds should mark these no-school days for childcare planning.
PWCS Spring Break (Mar 22–26) aligns with Fairfax County and Loudoun County. All three Northern Virginia districts share the same break week in 2026-2027.

Attendance boundaries in PWCS change frequently due to rapid growth. Verify your school assignment at pwcs.edu before signing a lease or purchasing a home — especially in Gainesville, Haymarket, and new Route 1 developments.
PWCS ends June 17 — the latest among Northern Virginia's large districts. Plan summer camp start dates and childcare for the third week of June.

districtFaqs.q: Does Prince William County observe Eid al-Fitr and Eid al-Adha as school holidays?
districtFaqs.a: Yes. Prince William County Public Schools observes Eid al-Fitr (March 10) and Eid al-Adha (May 17) as school holidays, along with Yom Kippur (September 21) and Juneteenth (June 18). This reflects the county's significant Muslim, Jewish, and African American communities. Families from other backgrounds should mark all of these as no-school days on their calendars.

livingHere.intro: Prince William County is the more affordable entry point into the Northern Virginia D.C. suburbs, attracting families priced out of Fairfax and Loudoun while still within commuting range of the Pentagon, Fort Belvoir, and downtown D.C. The county blends I-95 commuter suburbs with a significant military presence at Quantico and genuine Civil War history at Manassas.

highlights.label: I-95 Commuter Corridor
highlights.detail: The I-95 HOV lanes and Virginia Railway Express (VRE) commuter rail connect Woodbridge and Manassas to the Pentagon and D.C. — the VRE is the most practical option for families avoiding the 45–75 minute peak-hour I-95 backup.

highlights.label: Marine Corps Base Quantico
highlights.detail: MCB Quantico (including the FBI Academy) sits at the county's southern tip, generating a steady stream of military family transfers mid-year — PWCS is experienced with PCS enrollment and maintains liaison support for military families.

highlights.label: Most Affordable NoVA District
highlights.detail: Median home prices in Prince William run $150,000–$200,000 lower than Fairfax County, making it the most accessible entry point into Northern Virginia's school system for families on federal or military salaries.

highlights.label: Potomac Mills Outlet Mall
highlights.detail: One of the largest outlet malls on the East Coast sits in Woodbridge on Dale Boulevard — a major local employer and weekend shopping destination for the county's families and regional visitors.

highlights.label: Manassas Civil War Battlefields
highlights.detail: Two First and Second Bull Run battlefield parks adjoin the city of Manassas — a unique historical resource that PWCS schools use for field trips and that distinguishes the area from other NoVA suburbs.

highlights.label: Occoquan Reservoir & Water Supply
highlights.detail: The Occoquan Reservoir forms the county's northern boundary and is the drinking water source for much of Northern Virginia — surrounding parkland provides hiking, boating, and shoreline recreation close to suburban neighborhoods.
